text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic lavage, visible through the laparoscopic camera, to address the perforated diverticulitis.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate adhesions of the small bowel from the abdominal wall and the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to break up loculations within the abdominal cavity, aiming to separate and clear adhesions or fluid pockets.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the small bowel out of the pelvis to gain a clear view of the area, potentially identifying a perforation site for closure.
The surgeon reaches the pelvis anteriorly over the sigmoid colon and then shifts the view laterally.
The surgeon visually identifies the left pelvic side wall, examining its anatomical features and surrounding structures.
The surgeon has separated the small bowel from the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon, revealing the anatomical structures in the pelvic region.
The surgeon begins performing a lavage in the pelvic region, focusing on cleaning the area around the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon carefully explores deeper into the pelvic region, inspecting for any abscesses that may be contributing to the patient's urinary pressure and dysuria.
The surgeon inspects the area for any adherence of the sigmoid colon to the bladder, assessing the tissue connections between these structures.
The surgeon highlights the attachment of the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon to the right pelvic sidewall, indicating a point of interest in the surgical field.
The surgeon cleans up fibrinous exudate from the surgical area, having already drained the dominant areas.
The surgeon inspects the small bowel to ensure there are no inadvertent injuries in the areas previously separated using blunt dissection.
The surgeon positions the omentum into the surgical field.
The surgeon places a surgical drain into the pelvic cavity, ensuring proper positioning for fluid drainage.
The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery just distal to the takeoff of the left colic artery, using precise dissection techniques to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon takes the superior hemorrhoidal artery just distal to the takeoff of the left colic artery.
The surgeon performs medial dissection, focusing on the area near the previously isolated superior hemorrhoidal artery, distal to the left colic takeoff.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the area in the right pelvis that was previously adhered, preparing to dissect and mobilize the tissue.
The appendix is visibly drawn up into the surgical field, indicating its position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the appendix adherent to an abscess cavity and proceeds to remove it.
The surgeon ties off the structure using a silk tie instead of employing a staple load, demonstrating a manual ligation technique.
The surgeon attempts to bluntly separate tissue, focusing on the area previously identified as adherent to an abscess cavity.
The surgeon exposes an area with visible pus accumulation, indicating an infection or abscess in the surgical field.
The surgical field reveals anatomical structures near the iliac vessels and the right ureter.

The surgeon uses suction in the left hand to clear blood and other fluids from the surgical field, while retracting tissue. Dissecting scissors are held in the right hand for precise tissue manipulation.
The surgeon attempts to break through the residual rind of the abscess cavity to peel away the rectosigmoid colon.
The surgeon performs a dissection along the right rectal area, similar to a total mesorectal excision (TME), carefully navigating anterior to the abscess cavity to leave it on the sidewall.
The surgeon lifts the rectosigmoid colon up, showing it is now free from surrounding tissues after careful dissection.
The surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection, using instruments to carefully separate tissue layers adjacent to the previously exposed rectosigmoid area.
The surgeon performs medial dissection, progressing towards the splenic flexure without mobilizing it, as the flexure mobilization is deemed unnecessary in this case.
The surgeon isolates the recto sigmoid region in preparation for transection, focusing on the specific area for surgical intervention.
The surgeon performs a division slightly lower than the recto sigmoid, indicating a precise transection point in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates to the upper rectum to address adherence issues and avoid placing the anastomosis near the phlegmon.
The surgeon is dissecting the rectal mesentery, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect and take the proximal mesentery, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon shows the marked area on the tissue, indicating the desired margin for resection.
The surgeon advances the dissection of the mesentery towards the planned margin, revealing an area of demarcation a few centimeters proximal to the initial marking.
The surgeon makes a new mark on the tissue, indicating the planned margin for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon marks the rectum, aligning it with the previously established proximal margin.
The surgeon positions the sigmoid colon over the right iliac fossa and aligns it for a hand-sewn anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares to sew the back staple lines together, aligning the tissue edges for anastomosis.
The surgeon has mobilized below the peritoneal flexion in the pelvis, allowing the rectum to be elevated slightly.
The surgeon uses scissors to create a proctotomy and colotomy, visibly opening the rectum and colon for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to visualize the mucosa, highlighting the tissue with fluorescence for better assessment.
The surgeon begins suturing at the center of the surgical site, extending the stitches outward towards the far corner.
The surgeon begins suturing from the middle of the incision, moving towards the near corner, demonstrating the technique of closing the surgical site.
The surgeon performs suturing from the near corner away using a right-hand forehand technique, focusing on precise needle placement and thread tension.
The surgeon extends the suture across the surgical site, connecting it to the other suture line.
The surgeon ties two sutures together, completing the closure without taking additional throws.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to invaginate the anterior suture line, starting from the near corner and moving over the top of the back row.
The surgeon performs a leak test using rigid proctoscopy, visually inspecting the surgical site for any signs of leakage.
The surgeon inspects the surgical site using a rigid proctoscope, observing that the area appears clear with no signs of bleeding or leakage.
